The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable definitions and a single source entry pointing to a GitHub releases tarball. No top-level code execution or command substitution occurs when sourcing the file. The `pkgver()` function is not defined, so there is no dynamic version generation that could execute code. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are defined but will not be invoked during the source fetch and sourcing steps (due to `--nobuild --noprepare`). The source URL is the project's own release archive, and a checksum is provided, so no immediate risk from downloading or extracting the tarball. No obfuscated or suspicious code is present in the global scope. Therefore, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe.

The PKGBUILD follows standard packaging practices for an AUR package. - Source is downloaded from the project's own GitHub releases with a pinned checksum, so no tampering during fetch. - `prepare()` and `package()` perform straightforward extraction, file installation into `/opt`, creation of a symlink in `/usr/bin`, and installation of icons/desktop file. - The use of `strip` and deletion of `.debug` files is normal size optimization. - There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated commands, backdoors, or exfiltration attempts. - The self-provides/conflicts and the oddly large version number are unconventional but harmless, not evidence of malice.

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/zed-cn.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-xiqj3zjp...

Analyzing zed-cn AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-xiqj3zjp) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source download from a GitHub release URL using a direct HTTPS link: `https://github.com/TC999/zed-loc/releases/download/20260618/zed-linux-x86_64.tar.gz`. This is a static, versioned release asset, and the checksum is provided via `sha512sums`, which is appropriate for the source type. The `source` array does not use VCS (e.g., git+) so the use of a fixed checksum instead of 'SKIP' is correct.

The top-level scope of the PKGBUILD contains only safe variable assignments. There is no command substitution, function calls, or immediate execution of external commands at parse time. The `pkgver()` function is not defined, so no dynamic version calculation occurs during sourcing. Therefore, no code is executed when the PKGBUILD is sourced by `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or during source fetching with `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`.

The functions `prepare()` and `package()` are defined but will not be run under the specified flags (`--noprepare`, `--nobuild`). Thus, any potentially unsafe operations within them (e.g., `strip`, `cp -r`, `find -delete`) are irrelevant for this narrow audit. No obfuscated code, network exfiltration, or remote code execution is present in the top-level scope.

As a result, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` on this PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous code executes during source fetch or parsing
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code executes during source fetch or parsing[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD downloads a release tarball from the GitHub repository TC999/zed-loc, which is declared as the upstream source. The file is expected to contain a pre-built version of Zed with Chinese localization. The `sha512sums` array includes a checksum matching the source, which ensures integrity. The prepare() function extracts the tarball, and the package() function installs files into standard directories: `/opt/$pkgname` for the main application, symlinks into `/usr/bin`, icons into `/usr/share/icons`, and a desktop file into `/usr/share/applications`—all standard for AUR packages.

Operations such as `strip` on binaries, creating symlinks, installing icons, and setting up a desktop entry are routine and safe. The use of `find` to delete `.debug` files and stripping binaries is consistent with the `!debug` option to prevent debug package generation. There are no network requests during build or install, no use of `eval`, `curl`, or `wget` for dynamic code fetching, and no obfuscation. While the source is a pre-compiled binary (common in -cn packages), its checksum is verified, and the operations are confined to expected package management tasks.

All actions serve the clear purpose of installing a localized version of Zed and are consistent with AUR packaging standards. No data exfiltration, backdoors, or unauthorized external code execution is present. The package does not modify system files outside its scope or perform any suspicious runtime-like operations.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR package with verified pre-built binaries
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

LLM auditresponse for dev.zed.Zed-Dev.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.desktop` file is a standard desktop entry configuration used to integrate the Zed Devel editor into the desktop environment's application menu and file type associations. It declares common fields such as Name, Comment, Exec, Icon, and Categories, all pointing to the locally installed `zed` binary and assets. The `Exec` lines use straightforward calls to the `zed` command with safe, static arguments (`--new`, `%U` for URIs), with no use of shell evaluation, dynamic code loading, or external command execution.

There are no network operations, obfuscated commands, or risky scripts present. The MimeType entries (`text/plain`, `inode/directory` if uncommented, etc.) are typical for text editors and enable appropriate integration with the file manager. While there is a comment referencing a GitHub issue and a suggested workaround involving `gnome-defaults-list`, this is purely informational and not executable code. The use of HTML-escaped characters like `"` and `'` is correct and safe in this context.

A note on supply-chain risk: the file assumes the `zed` binary is securely installed and trusted. However, this desktop file itself does not introduce any malicious behavior, data exfiltration, or unauthorized system modifications. It operates entirely within the expected scope of application integration.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing src/zed-dev.app/licenses.md...

LLM auditresponse for licenses.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `licenses.md` contains only documentation of software licenses for various dependencies and assets used by the zed-cn package. It includes full text of permissive licenses such as MIT, Apache 2.0, Mozilla Public License 2.0, ISC, and others, applied to themes, icons, and code components. These are standard open-source licenses and their presence is normal for transparency and compliance.

No executable code, scripts, or commands are present in this file. The content is purely informational, listing copyright notices and license texts for third-party components. There are no network calls, file operations, base64-encoded payloads, eval statements, or any other suspicious patterns indicative of malicious activity.

Although the file is very large (over 32k lines), the head, tail, and sampled matching lines show only license boilerplate and URLs to legitimate upstream repositories (e.g., GitHub projects like Ayu, Gruvbox, Lucide, ICU4X). All such references are appropriate and verifiable. No evidence of obfuscation, backdoors, or supply-chain attacks is found.
</details>
<summary>
License file with no executable code or malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>
